ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for raw tapioca/cassava shoots as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this vegetable subtype is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw tapioca shoots is approximately 85 g (about 1 cup of loosely packed young shoots), commonly used as a vegetable side dish or in soups after blanching.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca shoots, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for tapioca shoots, raw (F14010030). Sodium, sugar, and saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry for cassava leaves, raw (NDB 11134 / FDC ID 170073) as the closest approved-source equivalent; adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for raw tapioca shoots. GI estimated by analogy to cassava leaves / non-starchy leafy vegetables with very low available carbohydrate (3.86 g/100g). A GI of 28 is applied based on the low-GI category anchor for non-starchy green vegetables reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 85 g serving = (28 × 3.86 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 0.9, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (11 mg/100g) with meaningful potassium (220 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (2.9 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Tapioca shoots are an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals: their very low sodium content and moderate potassium level support a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet principles, and their dietary fibre contributes to blood pressure reduction; however, they must be blanched or cooked before consumption to neutralise cyanogenic glycosides.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (3.86 g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.9 g/100g), and estimated low GI (~28)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.86 g available carbohydrate and 2.9 g dietary fibre per 100 g, tapioca shoots have a negligible glycaemic load (~0.9 per serving) and are suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; proper preparation (blanching or cooking) is essential to eliminate cyanogenic compounds before consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.09 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and meaningful potassium and vitamin C
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tapioca shoots are cardioprotective by virtue of their negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, meaningful dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and potassium and vitamin C contributing to vascular health; they should be cooked prior to consumption to remove cyanogenic glycosides.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols) despite low saturated fat and presence of vitamin C and calcium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tapioca shoots are low in saturated fat and provide vitamin C and calcium, but lack concentrated anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols; they are not contraindicated for RA but should not be relied upon as a primary anti-inflammatory food, and must be properly cooked to neutralise cyanogenic glycosides before consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tapioca shoots (also known as cassava shoots or pucuk ubi kayu) are the young, tender growing tips of the cassava plant (Manihot esculenta), harvested before the leaves fully mature. They are a traditional leafy vegetable widely consumed across Southeast Asia, particularly in Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and parts of Africa and South America. Raw tapioca shoots are characterised by a crisp texture and a mild, slightly bitter taste. They are almost always blanched, boiled, or stir-fried before eating to neutralise naturally occurring cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin and lotaustralin), which can release hydrogen cyanide if consumed raw in large quantities. After cooking, the shoots become tender and take on a mild, earthy flavour similar to spinach or water spinach. Nutritionally, tapioca shoots are a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable providing 36 kcal per 100 g, with 2.94 g protein, 0.37 g fat, and 3.86 g carbohydrate alongside 2.9 g dietary fibre. They are a meaningful source of calcium (60 mg/100g), iron (0.87 mg/100g), vitamin C (~52 mg/100g), and potassium (~220 mg/100g). Their very low sodium content (11 mg/100g) and low glycaemic index (~28) make them suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio supports gut health and glycaemic control. In Singaporean and Malaysian cuisine, cooked tapioca shoots are commonly served as a vegetable side dish, stir-fried with sambal, garlic, or belacan, or added to soups and curries.
common_user_queries: Are tapioca shoots safe to eat raw?
What are the health benefits of cassava shoots?
How do you cook pucuk ubi kayu?
Are tapioca shoots good for diabetics?
What is the nutritional value of cassava leaves?
Can I eat tapioca shoots if I have high blood pressure?
What vegetables are used in Southeast Asian cooking?
Are cassava shoots high in fibre?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (F14010030), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, saturated fat, fatty acids, vitamins, minerals) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central cassava leaves entry as the closest approved-source equivalent, with adaptation documented. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2021) / University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy leafy vegetables.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy 36 kcal, protein 2.94 g, fat 0.37 g, carbohydrate 3.86 g, dietary fibre 2.9 g, calcium 60 mg, iron 0.87 mg) are taken directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database entry F14010030 for tapioca shoots, raw. 2. Secondary nutrients (sodium 11 mg, sugar 0.6 g, saturated fat 0.09 g, monounsaturated fat 0.03 g, polyunsaturated fat 0.17 g, potassium 220 mg, vitamin C 52 mg, vitamin A 47 mcg RAE, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170073 (cassava leaves, raw) as the closest approved-source equivalent; values are consistent with the HPB-reported macros and represent the best available approved-source estimate for these micronutrients. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for raw plant food; no trans fats naturally present in cassava shoots). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant food contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero; negligible in raw leafy vegetables). 4. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy vegetables from Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL Values; no direct GI measurement for tapioca shoots exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. IMPORTANT FOOD SAFETY NOTE: Raw cassava shoots contain cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin, lotaustralin). Proper preparation — blanching, boiling, or cooking — is mandatory before consumption to reduce cyanide risk to safe levels. This record describes the raw nutritional composition; nutrient values will change modestly after cooking (particularly water-soluble vitamins). 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are applied as the best defensible ontology anchors for cassava shoots as a food item; these should be verified against the latest FoodOn and FoodEx2 release versions. 7. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context (very low carbohydrate, 3.86 g/100g) but is not a standard dietary_flags field; it is noted here for completeness.
